verses.text: Un peu plus de 200 ans auparavant, Abraham était descendu en Égypte lors d'une famine en Canaan ( **12.10** ), et Dieu l'y avait protégé. • Jacob s'arrête d'abord à Berchéba, où Abraham avait offert un sacrifice au Seigneur et l'avait adoré après avoir réglé un droit sur les terres et les eaux avec les Philistins ( **21.31-33** ). C'est également à cet endroit qu'Isaac avait vécu, et où Jacob lui-même avait résidé avant de fuir la colère d'Ésaü ( **28.10** ).

Dans une vision nocturne, le Seigneur a réitéré sa promesse d'accompagner Jacob et de faire de sa famille une grande nation en Égypte. Le même Dieu qui a conduit la famille en Égypte a promis de la faire sortir d'Égypte pour qu'elle vive à nouveau dans le pays de Canaan.

Dans une vision nocturne, le Seigneur a réitéré sa promesse d'accompagner Jacob et de faire de sa famille une grande nation en Égypte. Le même Dieu qui a conduit la famille en Égypte a promis de la faire sortir d'Égypte pour qu'elle vive à nouveau dans le pays de Canaan.
Il mourra paisiblement, entouré de sa famille et des nombreuses bénédictions de Dieu ( **49.33** ).

46:5 IMMIGRATION TO EGYPT. (Gen. 46:5-27) And Jacob rose up from Beer-sheba—to cross the border and settle in Egypt. However refreshed and invigorated in spirit by the religious services at Beer-sheba, he was now borne down by the infirmities of advanced age; and, therefore, his sons undertook all the trouble and toil of the arrangements, while the enfeebled old patriarch, with the wives and children, was conveyed by slow and leisurely stages in the Egyptian vehicles sent for their accommodation.

46:6 And they took their cattle, and their goods, which they had gotten in the land of Canaan,.... Some interpreters add, by way of explanation, and in Mesopotamia; much of Jacob's substance being yet there, though the greatest part was got in Canaan, and so that is put for the whole; and Jarchi supposes that Jacob gave all that he got in Padanaram to Esau for his part in the cave of Machpelah, and therefore mention is only made of his substance in Canaan;

46:7 daughters—As Dinah was his only daughter, this must mean daughters-in-law. all his seed brought he with him—Though disabled by age from active superintendence, yet, as the venerable sheik of the tribe, he was looked upon as their common head and consulted in every step.
Cette généalogie des fils de Jacob montre que toutes les tribus d'Israël sont allées ensemble en Égypte ; elles en repartiront également ensemble (voir le livre de l'Exode).\n\n

46:21 These are the sons of Bilhah, which Laban gave unto Rachel his daughter,.... To be her maid, when she was married to Jacob: and she bare these unto Jacob, all the souls were seven; not that she bare seven sons to Jacob, she bore but two, Dan and Naphtali; but the children of these with them made seven, one of Dan's, and four of Naphtali's, who went down with Jacob into Egypt.

46:22 All the souls that came with Jacob into Egypt,.... These are in parcels before mentioned, but here they are brought to a sum total; and by this phrase are excluded those that died before, as Er and Onan, and those that were in Egypt before, as Joseph and his two sons; and I should think also all that were born in Egypt afterwards, even while Jacob was living: those reckoned are only such: which came out of his loins: such as were his seed and offspring.

46:23 And the sons of Joseph, which were born in Egypt, were two souls,.... Ephraim and Manasseh; which is observed to show that they do not come into the above reckoning, but are to be taken into another that follows: all the souls of the house of Jacob, which came into Egypt, were threescore and ten; here it may be observed, the phrase is varied; it is not said, "all the souls which came out of the loins of Jacob", but "all the souls of the house" or family of Jacob;

46:24 And he sent Judah before him unto Joseph,.... Who was the more honourable of his sons, and in greater esteem with Jacob than his elder brethren were, Reuben, Simeon, and Levi, who by their conduct had greatly displeased him: moreover, he was a man of a polite address, and had endeared himself to Joseph by his speech to him, in which he discovered so much affection both to his father, and his brother Benjamin, and was upon all accounts the fittest person to be sent to Joseph: to direct his face unto Goshen;

46:25 And Joseph made ready his chariot,.... Or "bound" (y) it, fastened the horses to it, harnessed them, and put them to; this he did not himself, as Jarchi thinks, for the honour of his father; but rather, as Aben Ezra, by ordering his servants to do it: and went up to meet Israel his father in Goshen; that being higher than the other part of Egypt, as it must be, if it was in Thebes, or upper Egypt, as some Jewish writers say (z);

Il s'agit du nombre de ceux qui ont voyagé avec Jacob en Égypte, à l'exclusion des épouses de ses fils, des serviteurs et des autres personnes attachées à la maison. Il omet également Joseph, Éfraïm, Manassé et Jacob.

Le total de soixante-dix inclut Joseph, Éfraïm, Manassé et Jacob. Soixante-dix est aussi un nombre symbolique de perfection et d'achèvement (voir la note thématique " Nombres Symboliques" ). De ces soixante-dix (c'est-à-dire de tout Israël) naîtra la nation d'Israël qui bénira les soixante-dix nations (c'est-à-dire toutes les nations) du monde (voir la note d'étude sur Gn 10.2-32 ; cf. **12.3** ).
Jacob revit enfin son fils Joseph et leurs retrouvailles furent extrêmement joyeuses.\n\n
Joseph avait dix-sept ans lorsqu'il avait vu son père pour la dernière fois ( **37.2** ) ; il en avait trente-neuf à présent.

Jacob était comblé par le simple fait que son fils bien-aimé soit vivant, le premier-né de sa femme Rachel et chef de famille désigné (voir la note d'étude sur 48.5-7 ; voir aussi **1Ch 5.1-2** ). Cette réunion allait au-delà de simples retrouvailles familiales : elle prouvait que le plan de Dieu se déroulait comme prévu.

46:31 Joseph said, . . . I will go up, and show Pharaoh—It was a tribute of respect due to the king to inform him of their arrival. And the instructions which he gave them were worthy of his character alike as an affectionate brother and a religious man. Next: Genesis Chapter 47

46:32 The men are shepherds, for their trade hath been to feed cattle: Joseph coaches his brothers' answer to Pharaoh. *Roe tsoan* (keeper of flocks) was abominable to Egyptians (46:34), and Joseph turns the cultural stigma to his family's advantage — they will be granted Goshen, a region apart, which preserves their distinct identity through the generations of growth (Exod 1:7).

46:33 When Pharaoh shall call you, and shall say, What is your occupation?: Joseph anticipates the inevitable royal interview. The detail shows Joseph's wisdom for diaspora life — preparing his family to speak truthfully and shrewdly within a foreign court (cp. Dan 1:8; Esth 4:14). Pharaoh's question is the gate; their answer the key to Goshen.

Contrairement aux cananéens syncrétistes, qui auraient assimilé les Israélites s'ils étaient restés en Canaan, les Égyptiens détestaient les bergers sémites du fait de leur sentiment de supériorité ethnique et observaient une stricte ségrégation (voir **43.32** ). Lorsque la famille de Jacob s'est installée en Égypte, cette séparation a permis au peuple de devenir une grande nation sans perdre son identité.
